facts.text: The Appalachian Trail stretches approximately 2,190 miles from Springer Mountain in Georgia to Mount Katahdin in Maine, passing through 14 states.
The Pacific Crest Trail covers about 2,650 miles from the Mexican border to the Canadian border, traversing California, Oregon, and Washington.
The Camino de Santiago, or Way of St. James, is a network of pilgrimage routes leading to the Cathedral of Santiago de Compostela in northwestern Spain.
The Inca Trail to Machu Picchu is only 26 miles long but reaches a maximum altitude of 13,828 feet at Dead Woman's Pass.
The Continental Divide Trail runs 3,100 miles along the Rocky Mountains from Mexico to Canada, making it one of the longest trails in the U.S.
Grandma Gatewood, Emma Gatewood, became the first woman to solo thru-hike the Appalachian Trail in 1955 at the age of 67, wearing Keds sneakers.
The longest hiking trail in the world is the Great Trail in Canada, stretching over 15,000 miles from the Atlantic to the Pacific to the Arctic Ocean.
Leave No Trace is built on seven principles designed to minimize human impact on the outdoors, including packing out all trash and staying on marked trails.
Hiking just 30 minutes a day can reduce the risk of heart disease, lower blood pressure, and improve cholesterol levels.
The word 'hiking' comes from a dialectal English word meaning 'to walk vigorously,' first recorded in the early 19th century.
Mount Kilimanjaro in Tanzania is the tallest freestanding mountain in the world and can be summited without technical climbing equipment.
The Te Araroa Trail in New Zealand stretches 1,864 miles from Cape Reinga in the north to Bluff in the south.
Trekking poles can reduce the impact on your knees by up to 25% when hiking downhill.
The GR20 in Corsica, France, is widely considered one of the most difficult long-distance hiking trails in Europe.
A loaded backpack for a multi-day hike should ideally weigh no more than 20% of the hiker's body weight.
The Appalachian Trail Conservancy estimates it takes five to seven months to complete a thru-hike of the entire AT.
Hiking uphill burns roughly 400 to 550 calories per hour depending on terrain, pace, and pack weight.
The John Muir Trail in California runs 211 miles through the Sierra Nevada, passing through Yosemite, Kings Canyon, and Sequoia National Parks.
Properly broken-in hiking boots can prevent up to 90% of common blister problems on the trail.
The first known recreational hike is often attributed to the poet Petrarch, who climbed Mont Ventoux in France in 1336 purely for pleasure.
The Kokoda Track in Papua New Guinea is a 60-mile trail through dense jungle that was the site of a famous World War II battle.
Dehydration can set in after losing just 2% of your body weight in water, making hydration systems essential for hikers.
Hydration bladders, also called water reservoirs, allow hikers to drink hands-free through a tube while walking.
The average person walks about 100,000 miles in a lifetime, equivalent to circling the Earth four times.
Trail runners typically move at twice the speed of hikers, covering the same terrain in roughly half the time.
The highest paved road in North America is on Mount Evans in Colorado, reaching 14,130 feet, but hikers often prefer the summit trail.
AllTrails, one of the most popular hiking apps, has over 400,000 trail maps and has been downloaded by tens of millions of users worldwide.
The Pacific Crest Trail was designated a National Scenic Trail in 1968, though it wasn't officially completed until 1993.
Wearing moisture-wicking socks can reduce the risk of blisters by keeping feet dry and reducing friction.
The Appalachian Trail passes through Great Smoky Mountains National Park, the most visited national park in the United States.
Cairns, small stacks of rocks, have been used as trail markers for thousands of years across many cultures.
Japan's Kumano Kodo pilgrimage trail is one of only two pilgrimage routes in the world designated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, the other being the Camino de Santiago.
Trekking poles were originally adapted from ski poles and became popular for hiking in the 1970s in Europe.
The fastest known time for completing the Appalachian Trail is just over 41 days, set by Karel Sabbe in 2018.
Elevation gain is often a better indicator of trail difficulty than distance alone.
The Tongariro Alpine Crossing in New Zealand is considered one of the best day hikes in the world, crossing an active volcanic zone.
Gaiters are worn over boots and lower legs to keep debris, snow, and water out while hiking in rough terrain.
The Sierra Club, founded in 1892 by John Muir, is one of the oldest and largest hiking and environmental organizations in the United States.
Night hiking under a full moon can be a unique experience, as moonlight can illuminate trails enough to hike without a headlamp in open terrain.
The Long Trail in Vermont, completed in 1930, is the oldest long-distance hiking trail in the United States, running 272 miles.
A topographic map uses contour lines to show elevation changes, making it an essential navigation tool for backcountry hikers.
Declination, the difference between magnetic north and true north, must be accounted for when using a compass to navigate trails.
Thru-hiking refers to completing an entire long-distance trail in a single continuous journey, as opposed to section hiking.
Ultralight backpacking aims to reduce base pack weight to under 10 pounds, not counting food and water.
The Tour du Mont Blanc is a 110-mile trail that circles the Mont Blanc massif through France, Italy, and Switzerland.
Walking meditation, or kinhin, is a practice in Zen Buddhism where walking slowly and mindfully is a form of meditation.
The 10,000-steps-a-day guideline originated from a 1965 Japanese marketing campaign for a pedometer called Manpo-kei, meaning '10,000 steps meter.'
Switchbacks are zigzag paths on steep terrain that reduce the gradient and make climbing more manageable for hikers.
Flash floods are one of the most dangerous hazards in desert hiking, capable of filling dry washes in minutes.
The Haute Route from Chamonix to Zermatt is a classic Alpine trek covering about 112 miles with over 39,000 feet of total elevation gain.
Bear canisters are required in many national parks and wilderness areas to store food and prevent wildlife encounters.
The Kalalau Trail on Kauai, Hawaii, runs 11 miles along dramatic sea cliffs on the Na Pali Coast.
Hiking in high altitude requires acclimatization; ascending too quickly above 8,000 feet can cause altitude sickness.
The Pennine Way was the first official National Trail in England, established in 1965 and stretching 268 miles.
Waterproof breathable fabrics like Gore-Tex revolutionized hiking gear by keeping rain out while allowing sweat to escape.
The Triple Crown of Hiking consists of thru-hiking the Appalachian Trail, the Pacific Crest Trail, and the Continental Divide Trail.
Fewer than 500 people have completed the Triple Crown of Hiking, making it one of the rarest achievements in outdoor recreation.
GPS devices for hiking can pinpoint your location within about 10 feet, though tree cover and canyon walls can reduce accuracy.
Scotland's right to roam law allows hikers to walk across most private land as long as they act responsibly.
The Overland Track in Tasmania, Australia, is a 40-mile trail through Cradle Mountain–Lake St Clair National Park and is one of Australia's premier hikes.
Layering clothing is the most effective way to regulate body temperature while hiking: a base layer wicks moisture, a mid layer insulates, and an outer layer blocks wind and rain.
The Annapurna Circuit in Nepal is a classic Himalayan trek that reaches Thorong La Pass at 17,769 feet.
Hiking with a group is statistically safer than solo hiking, as injuries can be managed more effectively with assistance nearby.
The concept of National Scenic Trails in the United States was established by the National Trails System Act of 1968.
Vibram, the famous rubber sole company, was founded after Vitale Bramani lost six companions in a mountaineering accident due to poor footwear.
The W Trek in Torres del Paine National Park, Chile, is named for the W-shaped route it follows through Patagonian wilderness.
Desert hikers are advised to carry at least one gallon of water per person per day due to rapid dehydration in arid conditions.
Trail magic refers to unexpected acts of kindness on the trail, such as leaving water or food for thru-hikers at remote points.
The Milford Track in New Zealand was once called 'the finest walk in the world' and is a 33-mile route through Fiordland.
Birdwatching while hiking is called 'birding on foot' and is one of the fastest-growing outdoor activities combining two popular hobbies.
The Bibbulmun Track in Western Australia stretches 621 miles from Kalamunda near Perth to Albany on the southern coast.
Hypothermia can occur even in summer if hikers get wet and are exposed to wind at higher elevations.
Power walking is defined as walking at a pace of 4 to 5.5 mph, faster than normal walking but not running.
The Lycian Way in Turkey is a 335-mile trail along the Mediterranean coast, passing ancient ruins and turquoise bays.
A compass does not need batteries and is considered a more reliable backup navigation tool than GPS in the wilderness.
John Muir, often called the Father of the National Parks, walked over 1,000 miles from Indiana to Florida in 1867.
The Rim-to-Rim hike across the Grand Canyon is about 24 miles and drops nearly a mile in elevation before climbing back up.
DEET-based insect repellents are considered the gold standard for protection against ticks and mosquitoes on the trail.
Shinrin-yoku, or forest bathing, is a Japanese practice of slowly walking through forests to reduce stress and boost immunity.
The Zion Narrows in Utah require hikers to wade through the Virgin River, sometimes in waist-deep water, for much of the 16-mile route.
Step counters and pedometers date back to the 18th century, when Thomas Jefferson reportedly brought one from France to the United States.
The Laugavegur Trail in Iceland covers 34 miles through colorful rhyolite mountains, hot springs, and black sand deserts.
Carrying a whistle is recommended for all hikers; three short blasts is the universal distress signal.
The Great Wall of China stretches over 13,000 miles, and certain restored sections are popular hiking destinations.
Altitude sickness symptoms include headache, nausea, and dizziness, and the primary treatment is to descend to a lower elevation.
Urban walking tours have become a popular way to explore cities, with guided walks covering history, architecture, food, and street art.
The Appalachian Mountain Club, founded in 1876, is the oldest outdoor recreation and conservation organization in the United States.
A blaze is a painted mark on a tree or rock used to indicate a trail's route; the Appalachian Trail uses white blazes.
The Everest Base Camp trek in Nepal reaches 17,598 feet and typically takes 12 to 14 days to complete.
Merino wool is prized for hiking socks because it regulates temperature, wicks moisture, and resists odor naturally.
The Pacific Northwest Trail runs 1,200 miles from Montana's Glacier National Park to Washington's Olympic Coast.
Hikers should yield to horses and pack animals on trails, stepping to the downhill side and speaking calmly.
The Dolomites in Italy offer some of Europe's most dramatic hiking scenery, with jagged limestone peaks and via ferrata routes.
Via ferrata, meaning 'iron path' in Italian, are protected climbing routes using steel cables and ladders fixed to rock faces.
Walking just 20 minutes a day in nature has been shown to significantly reduce cortisol, the body's main stress hormone.
The Wonderland Trail encircles Mount Rainier in Washington, covering 93 miles with over 22,000 feet of elevation gain.
Naismith's Rule estimates hiking time by allowing one hour for every three miles of distance plus one hour for every 2,000 feet of ascent.
The Drakensberg Grand Traverse in South Africa covers about 140 miles along the escarpment of the Drakensberg Mountains.
Cotton clothing is avoided by experienced hikers because it retains moisture and loses insulating properties when wet, earning it the nickname 'death cloth.'
The Bruce Trail in Ontario, Canada, is the oldest and longest marked footpath in Canada at over 550 miles.
Lyme disease, transmitted by tick bites, is a significant concern for hikers in the northeastern and upper midwestern United States.
The Otter Trail along South Africa's Garden Route is a five-day coastal hike limited to 12 hikers per day to protect the environment.
The average walking speed for an adult is about 3.1 miles per hour on flat ground.
The concept of 'trail angels' — people who voluntarily help thru-hikers with rides, food, or lodging — is a beloved tradition of long-distance hiking culture.
The King's Trail (Kungsleden) in Sweden runs 270 miles through Arctic landscapes above the polar circle.
Headlamps are preferred over flashlights for hiking because they keep both hands free for balance and navigation.
Half Dome in Yosemite National Park requires a permit and the use of fixed cables to reach its 8,842-foot summit.
The GR10 traverses the French Pyrenees from the Atlantic to the Mediterranean, covering about 540 miles.
Moleskin is a common blister treatment that hikers apply directly to hot spots before blisters fully form.
The Israel National Trail runs about 683 miles from the northern tip of the country to the Red Sea in the south.
Geocaching, a GPS-based treasure hunting game, is a popular activity combined with hiking on trails worldwide.
The volcanic terrain of Hawaii Volcanoes National Park offers unique hiking over hardened lava flows and through lush rainforests.
A space blanket, weighing less than two ounces, can reflect up to 90% of body heat and is an essential emergency item for hikers.
The Chilkoot Trail in Alaska was used during the Klondike Gold Rush and is now a 33-mile historic hiking route.
Walking poles help distribute weight more evenly, effectively turning a two-legged activity into a four-legged one.
The Snowman Trek in Bhutan is considered one of the most difficult treks in the world, taking 25 days with passes above 16,000 feet.
Studies show that spending time walking in natural environments improves creative problem-solving by up to 50%.
The Routeburn Track in New Zealand is a 20-mile alpine crossing between Mount Aspiring and Fiordland National Parks.
Bear spray has been shown to be more effective than firearms at deterring bear attacks, stopping aggressive bears over 90% of the time.
The Hadrian's Wall Path in England follows the Roman-built wall for 84 miles from coast to coast.
Pedometers work by detecting the motion of a person's steps using a mechanical sensor or accelerometer.
The Inca Empire built over 25,000 miles of roads and trails, many of which are still used by hikers today.
Trail running shoes are lighter and more flexible than hiking boots but offer less ankle support on rough terrain.
The Heysen Trail in South Australia stretches 746 miles from Cape Jervis to the Flinders Ranges.
Sunburn risk increases by about 4% for every 1,000 feet of elevation gained due to thinner atmosphere.
The West Highland Way in Scotland covers 96 miles from Milngavie near Glasgow to Fort William at the foot of Ben Nevis.
Ankle sprains account for roughly 25% of all hiking-related injuries, making sturdy footwear a top safety priority.
The Appalachian Trail has over 250 shelters along its length, spaced roughly every eight miles for overnight hikers.
Camelback hydration packs popularized the hands-free drinking system and were originally developed for emergency medical workers.
The Larapinta Trail in Australia's Northern Territory runs 139 miles along the West MacDonnell Ranges.
Walking backwards, or retro-walking, is practiced in some fitness traditions and engages different muscle groups than forward walking.
The Camino de Santiago has experienced a massive surge in popularity, with over 400,000 pilgrims receiving the Compostela certificate in recent years.
Map and compass skills are collectively called orienteering, and competitive orienteering is recognized as a sport in over 70 countries.
The Torres del Paine O Circuit in Chile is an 80-mile loop that takes about 8 to 10 days to complete.
Hiking at a moderate pace can burn between 300 and 400 calories per hour for a 150-pound person.
The Sentiero degli Dei (Path of the Gods) on Italy's Amalfi Coast offers stunning coastal views along a cliff-side trail.
Lightning is a serious danger for hikers above treeline; the 30-30 rule suggests seeking shelter if thunder follows lightning by less than 30 seconds.
The South West Coast Path in England is the longest National Trail at 630 miles, following the coastline of Devon and Cornwall.
Smartwool, a popular hiking sock brand, helped bring merino wool back into mainstream outdoor gear in the 1990s.
A hiker's 'trail name' is a nickname adopted during a thru-hike, a tradition especially strong on the Appalachian Trail.
The Fish River Canyon Hiking Trail in Namibia is one of Africa's great hikes, traversing an 86-mile canyon over five days.
Water purification tablets containing chlorine dioxide can make backcountry water safe to drink in about 30 minutes.
Denali National Park in Alaska has only one road and vast trail-less wilderness, requiring hikers to navigate cross-country.
The Ueli Steck, known as the 'Swiss Machine,' set numerous speed records on Alpine climbing and hiking routes.
The average thru-hiker of the Appalachian Trail loses 20 to 40 pounds during the five to seven month journey.
Flânerie is the French art of leisurely walking through a city with no particular destination, simply observing urban life.
The Haute Randonnée Pyrénéenne traverses the Pyrenees for about 500 miles, often following the border between France and Spain.
Green Boots, a famous landmark on Mount Everest, is the body of a climber that serves as a grim waypoint near the summit.
Walking is the most popular form of exercise in the world, with billions of people walking daily for transportation or recreation.
The Bicentennial National Trail in Australia is the longest marked multi-use trail in the world at over 3,200 miles.
Ticks are most active in warm months and are commonly found in tall grass and leaf litter along hiking trails.
The Presidential Traverse in New Hampshire's White Mountains covers roughly 23 miles over peaks named after U.S. presidents.
The oxygen level at the summit of Mount Everest is about one-third of what it is at sea level.
Hiking with dogs is increasingly popular, but many trails and national parks require dogs to be on a leash at all times.
The GR5 trail runs from the North Sea at Hoek van Holland to the Mediterranean at Nice, covering about 1,550 miles.
REI Co-op was founded in 1938 by a group of Pacific Northwest climbers who wanted quality ice axes at fair prices.
The Jordan Trail stretches 400 miles from Um Qais in the north to Aqaba on the Red Sea.
Hiking downhill puts up to seven times your body weight in force on your knees, making trekking poles essential for descent.
In Japan, there is a tradition of climbing Mount Fuji at night to reach the summit in time for sunrise.
The first GPS satellite was launched in 1978, and the system became fully operational for civilian use in 1995, transforming wilderness navigation.
The Choquequirao Trek in Peru is an alternative to the Inca Trail, leading to ruins sometimes called the 'other Machu Picchu.'
Acute mountain sickness affects an estimated 25% of hikers who sleep above 8,000 feet for the first time.
The Great Glen Way in Scotland follows 79 miles of trails along a geological fault line from Fort William to Inverness.
Waterfall hikes are among the most popular day hikes in national parks, combining scenic beauty with relatively moderate difficulty.
Hiking in Sweden, Finland, and Norway benefits from the 'Right of Public Access,' which allows hiking on private land as long as no damage is done.
The concept of wearing red or bright colors while hiking helps with visibility and can aid search and rescue teams in emergencies.
The Inca Trail to Machu Picchu now requires permits, limited to 500 people per day including guides and porters.
The Seven Summits challenge involves climbing the highest peak on each continent, with several of them requiring extensive hiking approaches.
Strava and other fitness apps have popularized tracking hiking performance with GPS data, elevation profiles, and pace metrics.
The Peaks of the Balkans Trail is a 120-mile route through Albania, Kosovo, and Montenegro, crossing three countries.
Carrying a personal locator beacon (PLB) can send your GPS coordinates to search and rescue via satellite in an emergency.
National Parks in the U.S. receive over 300 million recreational visits per year, with hiking being the most popular activity.
The Toubkal Circuit in Morocco takes hikers to the summit of Mount Toubkal at 13,671 feet, the highest peak in North Africa.
The term 'trailhead' refers to the designated starting point of a hiking trail, often marked with a sign and a parking area.
Walking is considered a low-impact exercise that strengthens bones without the joint stress of running.
The Tahoe Rim Trail encircles Lake Tahoe over 170 miles, passing through both California and Nevada.
Frostbite can occur within 30 minutes when wind chill drops below minus 20 degrees Fahrenheit, making proper winter hiking gear critical.
The Via Dinarica is a long-distance trail running through the Dinaric Alps across eight countries in the Western Balkans.
Trail etiquette generally dictates that hikers going uphill have the right of way over those descending.
The Richtersveld Wildflower Trail in South Africa offers spectacular wildflower displays during the spring bloom.
Backpacking stoves weigh as little as three ounces and can boil water in under three minutes for on-trail meals.
The Kungsleden trail in Sweden passes through Abisko National Park, one of the best places in the world to see the Northern Lights.
Walking clubs have existed for centuries; the Sunday Tramps, a famous English walking club, was founded in 1879.
The average hiking speed on moderate terrain is about 2 miles per hour, accounting for elevation change and rest stops.
The Cotswold Way in England stretches 102 miles through picturesque English countryside and honey-colored stone villages.
Hypothermia can begin when core body temperature drops below 95 degrees Fahrenheit, even in conditions that seem mild.
The Mark O. Hatfield Wilderness in Oregon contains part of the Pacific Crest Trail and receives over 100 inches of rain per year.
Birdwatching is most productive during early morning hours, making dawn hikes ideal for spotting birds along the trail.
Walking pilgrimage routes exist in nearly every culture, from the Shikoku Pilgrimage in Japan to Aboriginal songlines in Australia.
The Maroon Bells near Aspen, Colorado, are the most photographed peaks in North America and offer several popular hiking trails.
Electrolyte replacement is essential during long hikes, as sweating depletes sodium, potassium, and magnesium.
The E1 European long-distance path stretches over 4,900 miles from Norway's North Cape to Sicily, Italy.
Micro-spikes or crampons are essential traction devices for hiking on icy or snowy trails during winter.
Crater Lake in Oregon, formed in the caldera of a collapsed volcano, is surrounded by over 90 miles of hiking trails.
The phrase 'take only photographs, leave only footprints' encapsulates the core philosophy of Leave No Trace hiking ethics.
Bushwalking is the Australian term for hiking, and is one of the most popular recreational activities in the country.
The South Kaibab Trail in the Grand Canyon descends 4,780 feet over just 7 miles, offering exposed ridgeline views the entire way.
Research shows that walking in nature reduces activity in the prefrontal cortex area associated with repetitive negative thoughts.
The Great Smoky Mountains have over 800 miles of trails, more than any other national park in the United States.
A survival kit for hiking should include at minimum a whistle, fire starter, emergency blanket, water purification, and a first aid kit.
The Cinque Terre trails along the Italian Riviera connect five colorful coastal villages and are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.
Rucking, or walking with a weighted backpack, has become a popular fitness trend adapted from military training.
The Samaria Gorge in Crete, Greece, is one of Europe's longest gorges and a popular 10-mile day hike.
The oldest hiking boots ever found were worn by Ötzi the Iceman, dating back over 5,300 years, and featured insulation of dried grass.
Satellite messengers like the Garmin inReach allow hikers to send text messages and SOS signals from anywhere on Earth.
The Zion Angels Landing trail features a chain-assisted scramble along a narrow ridge with 1,500-foot drop-offs on both sides.
Plantar fasciitis, inflammation of the foot's connective tissue, is one of the most common overuse injuries among frequent hikers.
Walking meditation is practiced in many Buddhist traditions, where each step is taken with full awareness of the movement.
The Colorado Trail stretches 486 miles from Denver to Durango, with an average elevation of over 10,300 feet.
Permethrin-treated clothing repels ticks and mosquitoes and remains effective through multiple washes.
The Foothills Trail in South Carolina covers 77 miles through the Blue Ridge Escarpment with numerous waterfalls.
Glacier National Park in Montana has over 700 miles of trails but is losing its glaciers rapidly due to climate change.
Japanese walking culture emphasizes 'ma,' or mindful pauses, turning a simple walk into a contemplative practice.
The O Treks and W Treks in Patagonia attract hikers from around the world seeking dramatic mountain and glacier scenery.
An emergency shelter or bivy sack weighing less than a pound can be a lifesaver if a hiker becomes stranded overnight.
The Nakasendo Trail in Japan is an ancient route connecting Kyoto and Tokyo through the mountainous Kiso Valley.
Hiking in thunderstorms above treeline is extremely dangerous; exposed ridges and summits are prime locations for lightning strikes.
The Wainwrights are 214 fells in the English Lake District described by Alfred Wainwright, and completing all of them is a popular hiking challenge.
Heel-to-toe foot placement is the most energy-efficient walking technique and reduces strain on joints.
The Cape Wrath Trail in Scotland is often called Britain's toughest long-distance walk, covering 200 miles of remote Highland terrain.
Banana slugs, found on Pacific Northwest trails, can grow up to 10 inches long and are important decomposers of forest floor debris.
The Indian Himalayas contain some of the highest trekking routes in the world, including the Chadar Trek on a frozen river.
Heel blisters form due to repetitive friction and can be prevented by properly laced boots and well-fitting socks.
The Fimmvörðuháls Trail in Iceland passes between two glaciers and crosses a lava field created by the 2010 Eyjafjallajökull eruption.
Nordic walking, which uses specially designed poles, engages 90% of the body's muscles compared to about 70% in regular walking.
Mount Washington in New Hampshire holds the record for the highest wind speed ever directly recorded on Earth's surface at 231 mph.
Section hiking allows people to complete long trails in segments over several years rather than all at once.
The Bob Graham Round is a famous fell running challenge in the Lake District covering 42 summits in 24 hours.
Water filters using hollow fiber membranes can remove 99.99% of bacteria and protozoa from backcountry water sources.
The Teton Crest Trail in Wyoming spans about 40 miles through the Teton Range with continuous views of jagged peaks.
Mud hiking or bog trotting is a niche hiking activity popular in parts of the UK and Scandinavia.
The Darien Gap between Panama and Colombia is one of the most dangerous and impassable hiking regions in the world.
Walking speed naturally decreases with age, but regular walking exercise can slow this decline significantly.
The Hayduke Trail is an 800-mile unofficial route through the canyonlands of southern Utah and northern Arizona.
Portable water purifiers using UV light can kill 99.99% of viruses, bacteria, and protozoa in just 60 seconds.
The Rota Vicentina in Portugal follows 280 miles of coastline through one of Europe's best-preserved coastal areas.
Trail runners consume an estimated 3,000 to 6,000 calories per day during ultra-distance events.
The Simien Mountains in Ethiopia offer trekking alongside endemic species like the gelada baboon and the Ethiopian wolf.
Cold weather hiking requires eating more calories because the body burns extra energy to maintain core temperature.
The Arizona Trail stretches 800 miles from Mexico to Utah, crossing deserts, forests, canyons, and mountain ranges.
The 'ten essentials' list for hiking was originally compiled by The Mountaineers in the 1930s and has been updated multiple times.
Walking barefoot on natural surfaces, called 'earthing' or 'grounding,' is believed by some to reduce inflammation and improve sleep.
The Overland Track in Tasmania requires booking months in advance during peak season and limits the number of hikers per day.
Black bears are generally more afraid of humans than humans are of bears and will retreat if given an escape route.
The Queen Charlotte Track in New Zealand is a 44-mile coastal walk through the Marlborough Sounds.
A trailhead register is a logbook at the start of a trail where hikers sign in, providing important safety data for rangers.
The Camino Portugués is a popular variant of the Camino de Santiago starting in Lisbon or Porto and heading north into Spain.
Seam-sealed rain jackets prevent water from leaking through the stitching, which is a common failure point in waterproof gear.
The Ala Archa Valley in Kyrgyzstan offers high-altitude trekking with views of glaciers and peaks over 15,000 feet.
Walking clubs in Germany, called Wandervereine, have existed since the early 1800s and maintain thousands of miles of trails.
Mountain goats on hiking trails are attracted to the salt in human sweat and urine, which can create dangerous encounters.
The Great Divide Trail in Canada runs 684 miles along the Continental Divide through the Canadian Rockies.
Bamboo trekking poles are used in many parts of Asia and are surprisingly strong and lightweight.
The Copper Canyon system in Mexico is deeper and larger than the Grand Canyon and offers remote multi-day hiking routes.
Walking at least 7,000 steps per day has been associated with a 50-70% lower risk of premature death in middle-aged adults.
Trail conditions can change dramatically with seasons; a summer scramble may become an impassable snow route in winter.
The Kerry Way in Ireland is the country's longest waymarked trail at 133 miles, looping around the Iveragh Peninsula.
Hammock camping has become an alternative to tent camping for hikers, especially in forests with abundant trees.
The Three Peaks Challenge in the UK involves climbing the highest peaks in Scotland, England, and Wales within 24 hours.
A UV protection rating of UPF 50+ in hiking clothing blocks over 98% of harmful ultraviolet rays.
The Pacific Coast Trail (not to be confused with the PCT) follows the Oregon coastline for 382 miles.
Volcanic hiking on active volcanoes like Mount Etna in Sicily requires monitoring eruption alerts and staying on approved routes.
The first Fitbit was released in 2009 and helped popularize step counting and daily walking goals among the general public.
The Pemi Loop in New Hampshire's White Mountains is a challenging 31-mile loop with over 9,000 feet of elevation gain.
The Te Araroa Trail in New Zealand includes sections where hikers must kayak or canoe along waterways.
Carabiner clip water bottles allow hikers to attach water to the outside of their packs for easy access.
The Kalalau Trail on Kauai requires a permit and is one of the most remote coastal hikes in the Hawaiian Islands.
The concept of forest kindergartens in Scandinavia involves children spending most of their school day walking and playing outdoors.
The Highline Trail in Glacier National Park traverses alpine meadows and offers some of the most spectacular views in Montana.
Hiking at altitude increases red blood cell production, which is why many endurance athletes train at elevation.
The Otter Trail in South Africa's Tsitsikamma section is so popular that bookings must be made up to a year in advance.
Charles Darwin's 'thinking path' at Down House, called the Sandwalk, was where he took daily walks to ponder his theories.
The concept of 'hike your own hike' (HYOH) encourages hikers to go at their own pace without judgment from others.
Mountain lions are rarely seen by hikers; there are fewer than 20 fatal mountain lion attacks recorded in North America in the last 100 years.
The number of people thru-hiking the Appalachian Trail has increased roughly tenfold since the 1990s.
Creek crossings are one of the most hazardous parts of a hike; unbuckling chest and waist straps before crossing is recommended in case of a fall.
The GR221, also called the Dry Stone Route, crosses Mallorca's Serra de Tramuntana mountain range for 84 miles.
The Walker's Haute Route from Chamonix to Zermatt is the hiking alternative to the mountaineering Haute Route.
The use of satellite imagery in apps like Gaia GPS allows hikers to see real-time terrain conditions before heading out.
Yosemite's Mist Trail to the top of Vernal Fall climbs 317 granite steps, earning it the nickname 'the Stairway of the Giants.'
The Kokoda Track in Papua New Guinea sees temperatures of up to 95°F with virtually 100% humidity.
Trailhead parking lots are common targets for vehicle break-ins; hikers should leave valuables at home or hidden.
The Confederation Trail on Prince Edward Island is a 273-mile rail trail that crosses the entire island.
Hiking improves balance and coordination, which are especially important for preventing falls in older adults.
The Haute Route Pyrénéenne stays as high as possible along the Pyrenees, often above 8,000 feet.
The United States has over 60,000 miles of National Recreation Trails available for public use.
Dehydrated and freeze-dried meals designed for backpackers just require boiling water and can weigh as little as 4 ounces dry.
The Markha Valley Trek in Ladakh, India, passes through remote Buddhist villages at altitudes over 16,000 feet.
Day hiking is the most popular form of hiking, with 80% of hikers preferring trips that require no overnight camping.
The Tiger's Nest Monastery in Bhutan is reached by a steep trail that climbs nearly 3,000 feet to a monastery perched on a cliff face.
Blisters are caused by friction, heat, and moisture — addressing any one of these three factors can prevent them.
In Australia, drop bears are a fictional creature used to prank tourists, but real hazards include venomous snakes and spiders on the trail.
The Pacific Crest Trail Association estimates that about 700 to 1,000 hikers attempt a PCT thru-hike each year.
Walking meditation labyrinths can be found in parks, churches, and hospitals worldwide as tools for contemplation and stress relief.
The Appalachian Trail has an estimated 3 million visitors per year, though only about 3,000 attempt a thru-hike annually.
Giardia, a microscopic parasite found in backcountry water, causes severe gastrointestinal illness and is the main reason hikers purify water.
The Ridgeway in England is called Britain's oldest road, following chalky paths that have been used for at least 5,000 years.
Thru-hikers on the Appalachian Trail collectively eat an estimated 4 to 6 million calories per thru-hike.
The Salkantay Trek in Peru is an alternative to the Inca Trail that reaches 15,213 feet at Salkantay Pass.
The Japanese concept of 'komorebi' describes sunlight filtering through leaves, a sight frequently enjoyed by forest hikers.
Satellite phones can cost several dollars per minute but remain the only communication option in extremely remote hiking areas.
The GR34 in Brittany, France, follows the entire Breton coastline for over 1,100 miles.
Proper foot care on the trail includes airing out feet during rest stops and changing into dry socks.
The Presidential Range in New Hampshire's White Mountains is known for some of the worst weather in the world.
The Camino del Norte follows Spain's northern coast for about 500 miles and is a less crowded alternative to the main Camino route.
Hiking with children under five typically limits the pace to about one mile per hour, and trails should be chosen for safety.
Mountain huts, or refugios, in the Alps provide food, shelter, and beds for hikers without the need to carry camping gear.
Trekking in Nepal directly supports local economies, as teahouse lodges along major routes are family-run businesses.
The Inca Trail's permit system was introduced in 2001 after concerns about trail degradation from too many visitors.
Walking the entire coastline of Great Britain (the England Coast Path) will be approximately 2,795 miles when fully completed.
The Cerro Torre trek in Patagonia leads to views of one of the most technically difficult peaks in the world.
A natural walking gait involves the arms swinging opposite to the legs, which improves balance and energy efficiency.
The Annapurna Base Camp trek is a shorter alternative to the full circuit, reaching 13,549 feet in about 7 to 12 days.
Wearing a hat while hiking can reduce heat loss through the head by up to 10% in cold conditions and prevent heat stroke in warm weather.
The Waitukubuli National Trail in Dominica is the longest hiking trail in the Caribbean at 115 miles.
Traction cleats like Yaktrax or Kahtoola Microspikes can transform regular hiking boots into ice-capable footwear.
The South Downs Way in England runs 100 miles along England's newest national park.
About 75% of people who start a thru-hike of the Appalachian Trail do not finish, often due to injury, weather, or burnout.
The highest trail in Europe is on Mont Blanc, with the normal route summiting at 15,781 feet.
Wilderness permits are required in many U.S. wilderness areas to limit visitor numbers and protect the environment.
The Offa's Dyke Path follows the Anglo-Welsh border for 177 miles along an earthwork built 1,200 years ago.
The Huemul Circuit in Patagonia involves river crossings by zip line and is considered one of the most adventurous treks in Argentina.
Wrist-mounted altimeters measure atmospheric pressure changes to estimate elevation, helping hikers track their progress on mountain trails.
The Inca Trail to Machu Picchu passes through three distinct ecological zones: cloud forest, highland, and alpine tundra.
Walking tours are the fastest-growing segment of the tourism industry, outpacing beach and resort vacations in recent years.
The Lost Coast Trail in California is one of the few remaining wilderness coastlines in the state, stretching 25 miles.
Pole planting technique matters: the tip should contact the ground slightly behind the body's center of gravity for maximum benefit.
The Simien Mountains trek in Ethiopia is one of the few places to see the rare Walia ibex in its natural habitat.
The Slovenian Mountain Trail was one of the first long-distance mountain trails in Europe, established in the 1950s.
Cairn building by hikers is discouraged in many areas because it disrupts trail markers and can harm fragile ecosystems.
In Italian, a leisurely evening walk is called a 'passeggiata,' and it is a deeply embedded cultural tradition in many Italian towns.
The John Muir Way in Scotland stretches 134 miles from Helensburgh to Dunbar, the birthplace of John Muir.
Compression socks can reduce leg fatigue and swelling during long hikes by improving blood circulation.
The Trans-Catalina Trail on Catalina Island off the California coast covers 38 miles across the island's rugged interior.
The Abel Tasman Coast Track in New Zealand features golden beaches and turquoise water along 37 miles of coastline.
Walking at least 4,000 steps per day has been shown to reduce the risk of dying from any cause in adults.
The Lares Trek in Peru passes through traditional Quechua communities and offers cultural experiences not found on the Inca Trail.
The South West Coast Path in England has total ascent and descent roughly equivalent to climbing Mount Everest four times.
Down insulation provides more warmth per ounce than synthetic insulation but loses its insulating properties when wet.
The Jebel Shams Balcony Walk in Oman follows a narrow path along the rim of Oman's Grand Canyon.
Many hikers practice 'Leave No Trace' by bringing a small trowel to bury human waste at least 200 feet from water sources and trails.
GPS watches designed for hiking can track elevation gain, distance, heart rate, and barometric pressure in real time.
The Chesapeake and Ohio Canal towpath provides 184 miles of flat hiking and biking from Cumberland, Maryland, to Washington, D.C.
Walking speed is used as a clinical measure of overall health; a pace below 0.8 meters per second is associated with higher health risks.
The Fitz Roy Trek in Patagonia features stunning views of the iconic Fitz Roy massif and its surrounding glacial lakes.
A properly adjusted hip belt on a backpack should carry about 80% of the pack's weight on the hiker's hips rather than shoulders.
The Cape to Cape Track in Western Australia, stretching 75 miles along the coast, passes through karri forests and limestone caves.
The number of hiking-related search and rescue operations in U.S. national parks exceeds 3,000 per year.
Walking poles made from carbon fiber are lighter than aluminum poles but more susceptible to breaking under sudden impact.
The Shikoku Pilgrimage in Japan comprises 88 temples connected by a 750-mile walking route completed by thousands of pilgrims each year.
Crevasses on glacier hikes can be hidden by snow bridges, making rope teams and route-finding skills essential.
The Camino Frances, the most popular route of the Camino de Santiago, stretches about 500 miles from St-Jean-Pied-de-Port in France to Santiago de Compostela.
Ramblers, the UK's largest walking charity, campaigns for access to green spaces and maintains a network of paths across Britain.
Sun protection while hiking includes sunscreen, sunglasses with UV protection, and a wide-brimmed hat.
The use of handheld GPS units on trails became widespread in the early 2000s, gradually replacing map-and-compass for many day hikers.
Night hiking requires red-light headlamps to preserve night vision while still illuminating the trail.
The Haute Savoie region of France is home to some of the most popular day hikes in the Alps.
Virtual hiking challenges and step competitions have grown in popularity, motivating millions to walk more using fitness trackers.
The Dingle Way in Ireland is a 111-mile loop around the Dingle Peninsula with views of the Atlantic Ocean.
A blister kit should include moleskin, athletic tape, antiseptic wipes, and a sterile needle for draining if necessary.
Mount Whitney in California, the highest peak in the contiguous U.S. at 14,505 feet, can be summited via a 22-mile day hike.
The Appalachian Trail features more than 165,000 white blazes painted on trees and rocks to guide hikers along the route.
The Cami de Cavalls in Menorca, Spain, is a 115-mile coastal path that circles the entire island.
Hemlock woolly adelgid, an invasive insect, is devastating hemlock trees along many eastern U.S. hiking trails, changing forest landscapes.
In Nepal, 'namaste' is the traditional greeting exchanged between trekkers and locals on Himalayan trails.
The Dolomites Alta Via 1 is a 75-mile trail through Italy's Dolomite range, typically completed in 10 to 12 days.
Hiking boots with ankle support became the standard for serious hiking in the mid-20th century, though many now prefer lighter trail shoes.
The St. Olav Ways are pilgrimage routes in Scandinavia leading to Trondheim, Norway, inspired by medieval pilgrims.
Walking aids recovery from many chronic conditions, and doctors increasingly prescribe 'park prescriptions' for patients to walk in nature.
The Perito Moreno Glacier in Argentina offers ice trekking where hikers walk on the surface of a moving glacier with crampons.
Cell phone coverage in many wilderness areas is nonexistent, making offline maps and GPS devices essential safety tools.
The Pieterpad in the Netherlands is the country's most famous long-distance path, covering 310 miles from north to south.
Trekking in the Karakoram range of Pakistan includes the K2 Base Camp Trek, often called one of the greatest treks on the planet.
Ultraviolet exposure while hiking at high altitude can cause snow blindness, a painful temporary condition prevented by proper sunglasses.
The Ice Age Trail in Wisconsin stretches over 1,200 miles, following the edge of the last continental glacier.
The world record for the most miles walked in a year is over 22,000, achieved by extreme endurance walkers.
Weighted vest walks are a popular alternative to rucking for hikers who want to increase cardiovascular intensity without a backpack.
The pilgrim passport, or credencial, is stamped at various points along the Camino de Santiago and is required to receive the Compostela certificate.
The Highlands of Iceland offer some of the most remote and Mars-like hiking terrain on Earth, with volcanic landscapes and hot springs.
